step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='UP - both squares aligned with corridors above')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 24, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have the full maze structure. Let me note the black corridors (excluding the squares themselves):
Rows 9-13: cols 19-48 (full bar at top - 30 cells wide)
Rows 14-18: cols 14-28 (left, 15 cells), cols 34-53 (right, 20 cells)
Rows 19-23: cols 9-23 (left, 15 cells), cols 39-53 (right, 15 cells)
Rows 24-28: cols 9-18 (left, 10 cells), cols 44-53 (right, 10 cells)
Rows 29-33: cols 49-53 (right only, 5 cells) - NOTE: no left corridor here!
Rows 34-38: cols 14-18 (left, 5 cells), cols 49-53 (right, 5 cells)
Rows 39-43: cols 9-23 (left, 15 cells), cols 44-48 (right, 5 cells)
Rows 44-53: cols 9-28 (left, 20 cells), cols 34-53 (right, 20 cells)
Rows 54-58: cols 9-28 (left, 20 cells), cols 34-48 (right, 15 cells)
Wait, but rows 29-33 only show cols 49-53 as black. But the left square is currently at rows 29-33, cols 14-18. Looking at the output, rows 29-33 don't have a left corridor. But the square is there... Let me re-check.
Actually, the ASCII output shows the current frame, which includes the squares. The squares are sky blue (S), not black (B). So the scan for B segments doesn't include the squares. This means:
- Rows 29-33: only cols 49-53 are black (right corridor), no left corridor
- But the left square is at rows 29-33, cols 14-18
Wait, that's strange. The left square is at rows 29-33, cols 14-18, but there's no black corridor there. How did it get there? Looking at the earlier output, rows 34-38 have cols 14-18 as black (left corridor). The square moved up from rows 34-38 to rows 29-33. But rows 29-33 don't have a black corridor at cols 14-18...
Unless the square is ON TOP of the corridor, replacing the black cells with sky blue. That makes sense - the square occupies the corridor space. So the actual corridor at rows 29-33 includes cols 14-18 (where the square is) and cols 49-53.
Let me re-examine. The square is 5x5. When it's at rows 29-33, cols 14-18, those cells show as S (sky blue) in the ASCII, not B (black). So the scan for B segments misses the square's position.
So the actual maze structure is:
Rows 29-33: cols 14-18 (left, 5 cells - occupied by square), cols 49-53 (right, 5 cells)
Rows 34-38: cols 14-18 (left, 5 cells), cols 49-53 (right, 5 cells)
This means the left corridor at rows 29-38 is cols 14-18 (10 rows high, 5 cells wide).
And the right corridor at rows 29-38 is cols 49-53 (10 rows high, 5 cells wide).
Now, the right square is at rows 39-43, cols 49-53. To move up, it needs to enter the corridor at rows 34-38, cols 49-53. The square is at cols 49-53, which matches the corridor above. So UP should work!
Let me also check the left square. It's at rows 29-33, cols 14-18. Above it is rows 24-28, which has a left corridor at cols 9-18. The square at cols 14-18 is within cols 9-18, so UP should work for the left square too.
Let me try UP!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Full maze mapped. Current:
# Left: rows 29-33, cols 14-18
# Right: rows 39-43, cols 49-53
#
# Above left (rows 24-28): left corridor cols 9-18 (includes 14-18 ✓)
# Above right (rows 34-38): right corridor cols 49-53 (matches 49-53 ✓)
# Both can go UP!
predict(expect_change=True, note='UP - both squares aligned with corridors above')
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Blue: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Blue: rows 24-28, cols 14-18
Blue: rows 34-38, cols 49-53
